title: Computer Facilities Management Services

narrative.executive_view: Computer facilities management is a poor broad roll-up candidate despite meaningful automatable work. The relevant operator manages a client's existing IT estate; it is not a cloud host or an SMB systems integrator. ISG reports only 2.1% managed-services growth in 2026 while cloud XaaS is forecast to grow 30%, and it describes a market where labour-intensive work is being displaced by LLMs [S1]. That mix makes the surviving regulated, legacy and sovereignty-sensitive estate a niche rather than evidence that the 2022 revenue base is durable.
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: AIOps can take first-pass monitoring, event correlation, runbook execution, patching coordination, ticket handling, capacity reporting and much of SLA reporting out of delivery teams. Kyndryl's Advanced Delivery initiative is explicitly AI-enabled automation, and its newer Agentic Service Management is intended to enable autonomous IT operations at scale [S3]. The assigned role fractions therefore give high but not total exposure to support and the unlisted operations tail; physical hands, incident accountability, client governance and constrained change windows remain human work. The supplied four-digit 5415 mix overstates software developers for this particular code, so the developer fraction is deliberately restrained.
narrative.value_capture: The central issue is not whether a provider can remove delivery hours, but who receives the gain. ISG says providers face pricing deflation and provider-funded AI transformation embedded in contracts [S1]; its deal analysis says providers price expected AI savings into consolidation-led TCO deals and often pull those savings forward for clients [S2]. A multi-year incumbent can retain a temporary execution gain, but procurement benchmarking, fixed run-rate commitments and renewal negotiations give the customer considerable claim on it. The 25% distribution estimate and 30% moat estimate consequently treat capture as weak, not as a reward for the human floor that keeps service demand alive.
narrative.adoption_timing: There is clear deployment by leading providers, rather than proof that half of lower-middle-market facilities operators have achieved effective autonomous operations. Kyndryl launched Agentic Service Management during fiscal 2026 [S3], while ISG says AI economics are already shaping managed-service deal structures but also that automation and modernization take time [S2]. I estimate 25% current effective adoption of the target task bundle and three years to 50%, with low confidence. The estimate is intentionally not inferred from cloud growth or from the number of buyers, which are different mechanisms.
narrative.consolidation_economics: The frozen $1–10M EBITDA band contains 583 firms, but the code is unusually skewed toward large enterprises and may include subsidiaries or captive units rather than independent acquisition targets. ISG observes that enterprises are consolidating spend with major providers for cost takeout while using small specialists for narrow projects; undifferentiated mid-spend capacity is the difficult middle [S2]. No fresh, code-specific lower-middle-market transaction set or owner-demographic source was found in this run. Buyability and multiple selections are therefore held conservative and source-free rather than padded with MSP, hosting or broad IT-services proxies.
narrative.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is MELTING for the broad code, although a paid residue can persist. Cloud XaaS growth is greatly outpacing managed services, with IaaS a major driver [S1], so client workload migration is a structural alternative to paying a third party to operate legacy facilities. In parallel, AI reduces the labour intensity of the operation that remains. Regulated, legacy and sovereign workloads can require accountable operators, but that narrow residue does not validate durable demand for the generic facilities-management base or turn client-owned operations into provider-owned hosting.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: This is a heterogeneous code with a low labour-share input because receipts can include hardware, software, capacity and subcontractor pass-through. The role mix is a four-digit 5415 proxy, so both the operational tail and the developer line are uncertain. The most material unobserved inputs are the local contract mix, effective adoption and whether a nominal target is truly independent rather than a captive or a hosting business. Pricing evidence comes from global managed-services deal research and Kyndryl, not a statistically representative sample of $1–10M EBITDA facilities operators; that scope limitation drives the LOW overall confidence.

sources.title: Global Tech Services Market Grows at Fastest Pace Ever in Q2, Propelled by Soaring AI Demand: ISG Index
sources.what_it_supports: 2026 managed-services growth, ITO direction, the much faster cloud XaaS/IaaS market, labour-intensive work displaced by LLMs, pricing deflation and provider-funded AI transformation in contracts.

sources.title: Index Insider | Scope, Shape and Strategy: AI's Impact on Deal Structures in 2026
sources.what_it_supports: Managed-services consolidation, client TCO objectives, providers pricing expected AI savings into deals, cost smoothing, and the pressure on undifferentiated mid-spend providers.

sources.title: Kyndryl reports fourth quarter and full-year 2026 results
sources.what_it_supports: A current large managed-infrastructure provider's Advanced Delivery AI automation and launch of Agentic Service Management for autonomous IT operations.

sources.title: Index Insider | 1H26 ISG Index Recap
sources.what_it_supports: First-half 2026 contrast between muted managed-services growth and strong IaaS growth, the negative AI expectation for labour-centric managed services, and the market's larger-provider consolidation pattern.

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541513 ($11,535,095,371) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541513 ($30,832,208,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

occupations.title: Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel
Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $266,793 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541513); class <5: 3,040 firms, avg revenue/firm $354,248 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $55,440 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 692 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,447,064 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $226,465 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 484 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,262,322 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $510,553 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 429 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,791,140 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,375,813 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 154 firms, avg revenue/firm $37,324,773 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,841,327 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 151 firms, avg revenue/firm $116,924,159 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $18,298,631 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 583
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: AIOps and autonomous service-management evidence supports substantial automation of monitoring, service desk and runbook work, while physical operations, accountability and client governance bound the task-level estimates.
ai_replaceable_share.plausible_range: 0.32-0.56 of total wage share after role weighting
caveats: The frozen mix is four-digit 5415 rather than 541513, and its software-developer line is not a clean proxy for facilities operations.
Do not treat provider product launches as a direct survey of task automation at lower-middle-market firms.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Documentation, scripting and routine remediation automate, but the supplied developer occupation is only a partial proxy for this code's operations core.
Tier-one and repeat ticket workflows are strong AIOps and agent-assist targets; exception handling and account context remain.
SLA reporting and internal coordination automate, but customer escalation and operational accountability remain human.
Reporting, configuration analysis and transition documentation are partly automatable; client-specific change design persists.

breakdown_by_role.role: Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Proposal support automates, but long-cycle outsourcing sales and renewals depend on relationships and risk allocation.
Back-office planning and reporting automate, while management of service commitments and delivery exceptions persists.
The broad computer-occupations line likely contains monitoring and operations work exposed to runbook automation, but the title is not code-specific.
Status production, scheduling and coordination automate partly; transition governance and stakeholder resolution remain.
Analysis and recurring reporting are automatable, but commercial and client advisory judgment remains.

breakdown_by_role.role: Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Test preparation and regression work automate, but incident-risk validation and customer acceptance remain.
The unlisted tail likely combines shift operations and back office exposed to AIOps with physical facilities work that remains on site.

pi_dist.rationale: Contract-level productivity commitments, TCO cost-reduction objectives and pricing of expected AI savings mean a provider may keep some in-term execution gain but likely passes most economics to clients through price and renewal terms.
caveats: ISG evidence is global managed services, not a lower-middle-market 541513 contract sample.
The selection concerns allocation of savings, not the amount of automatable work.

pi_moat.rationale: Transition risk and account knowledge create some incumbency, but buyer cost-takeout objectives, provider consolidation and pricing deflation leave a weak moat for generic facilities management.
caveats: A regulated or sovereign specialist can be materially stronger than this broad-code estimate.
Human accountability supports residual demand but is not counted as automatic pricing power.

t50_years.rationale: Leading providers are launching autonomous-operations offerings, but ISG says automation and modernization take time; three years is a bounded judgment to reach 50% effective use of the operational task bundle.
caveats: No source reports 541513 lower-middle-market effective-adoption penetration or a direct t50 estimate.
This is separate from cloud migration, which changes the addressable base rather than the rate of AI adoption.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: Current provider deployments and agentic-service launches show meaningful but not majority effective adoption in the target task bundle; 25% is deliberately below the 50% boundary.
caveats: Product availability and large-provider deployment are not equivalent to realized adoption at $1–10M EBITDA operators.

historical_analogs.value: Cloud-era managed-services consolidation and modernization: providers and clients have used longer TCO-oriented contracts to fund lower run rates, while providers with generic labour capacity are squeezed.
historical_analogs.rationale: The current ISG discussion documents cost-takeout consolidation, longer-term contracts and concurrent cloud expansion, furnishing a directly relevant directional analogue rather than a quantified adoption curve.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Directional analogue only; it does not set t50 mechanically.
caveats: The sources describe current market structure rather than a longitudinal, code-specific history.

owners_60plus_pct.rationale: No dedicated age-of-owner evidence was found for this large-firm-skewed code; a low, non-zero placeholder avoids claiming a succession tail that is not evidenced.
caveats: This is not a measured 541513 owner-age statistic and should not be used to infer seller supply.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: This run found no code-specific documentation of a succession shortage, so the buyability uplift is not claimed.
succession_shortage_documented.plausible_range: False unless code-specific evidence is supplied.
caveats: Absence of evidence is not evidence of no shortage; it only prevents a positive score adjustment.

active_consolidators.rationale: No fresh, code-specific record of active lower-middle-market 541513 consolidators was validated; zero is a conservative scoring assumption rather than a claim that no buyer exists.
caveats: Broad IT-services, MSP and provider-owned hosting deals are excluded because they cross the stated industry boundary.

buy_mult.rationale: Without a verified target-level transaction set, a cautious mid-single-digit buy assumption is used solely to test whether a melting, weak-capture business has an expansion case.
caveats: Not an observed 541513 transaction multiple; hosting and MSP comparables were intentionally not used.

exit_mult.rationale: The exit case assumes a discount to entry if the target remains generic labour-based facilities management under cloud and AI pressure.
caveats: Not an observed 541513 transaction multiple; a differentiated regulated niche could exit differently.

pricing_structure.value: Multi-year managed-service contracts with TCO cost-reduction targets, provider-funded modernization and pricing that embeds expected automation savings; actual lower-middle-market mixes may also include per-seat, per-device and fixed-fee schedules.
pricing_structure.rationale: ISG directly describes cost-reduction-led contracts and pricing of AI savings, so the structure is treated as deflationary for the operator rather than as a generic recurring-revenue moat.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Contract mix varies materially by client estate and renewal cycle.
caveats: No survey of 541513 contract-form revenue weights was located; this must not be read as a measured mix.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: The role-weighted labour opportunity is meaningful, while the three-year t50 estimate reflects rapid provider product progress but delayed effective use across client estates; neither cloud migration nor seller supply is used to set A.

terminal_value.justification: Cloud XaaS growth far exceeds managed-services growth and AI removes labour intensity from the remaining operation. Regulated, legacy and sovereignty-bound estates may persist, but they are a narrower niche than the broad code.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: No proxy-derived ownership, buyer-count or transaction multiple is presented as a code-specific fact. Broad global managed-services sources are limited to automation, pricing and market-direction context; provider-owned hosting and SMB MSP comparables are excluded.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: The same contract mechanism is used only in C: expected automation savings are priced into TCO deals and passed to clients. It is not used to raise V, A, B, M or terminal demand.

dataset_mismatch.description: The frozen role mix is four-digit 5415 and the labor-share input combines 2024 wages with 2022 receipts; 541513 may have more operations and physical-facilities work than the supplied occupations show.
sensitivity: A higher physical-operations share would lower the role-weighted AI-replaceable share and V.
Pass-through-heavy receipts make labor intensity and the economic meaning of a single code-wide margin highly variable.

subsegments: Client-owned IT estate and facilities operation
Regulated, legacy and sovereignty-sensitive outsourced operations
Provider-owned cloud, data-center or hosting capacity
SMB managed service providers and systems integration
market_boundary.dominant_subsegment: Client-owned, ongoing outsourced computer-systems and facilities operations.
market_boundary.selection_basis: The target boundary excludes 518210-style provider-owned hosting, 541512-style systems design/SMB MSP work and 541511 programming. The terminal judgment is about the client-owned operations base rather than cloud-provider demand.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A ranges from 6.67 to 2.00; it cannot overcome the weak capture and exit case, but it changes the pacing of operating pressure.
C changes directly with the share of savings retained after client pricing and renewal terms.
V varies with the unobserved mix between AIOps-exposed operational work and physical or accountable work.
The M factor remains zero below the 4.0x entry assumption and only becomes positive if a differentiated exit multiple is demonstrated.

reviewer_note: Fresh 2026 managed-services and provider evidence supports the directional thesis, but no code-specific lower-middle-market contract, ownership or transaction dataset was found. This is intentionally a transparent low-confidence KILL rather than a proxy-filled roll-up case.
